answers:
**1. Root cause.**
`db/migrations/0003_rls.sql` correctly enables RLS and adds a `tenant_isolation` policy on `appointments` keyed on `current_setting('app.current_tenant', true)::uuid`, and `backend/app/deps.py:13-21` correctly pins that setting per-request from the verified JWT before any query runs. However, in Postgres, **the table owner bypasses row-level security policies by default**, regardless of the `BYPASSRLS` role attribute, unless the table has `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` set — and `0003_rls.sql` never sets `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` on `appointments`. The application's runtime `DATABASE_URL` in `.env.example:1`, `docker-compose.yml:5`, and `infra/main.tf:2` all point at `juniper_owner` — the exact role that ran the migrations and therefore owns the `appointments` table (per the role-purpose comment at `db/migrations/0001_init.sql:1-5`). Because the runtime connection is the table owner, every query the API issues — including the plain `SELECT ... FROM appointments` in `backend/app/routes/appointments.py:16-19`, which has no application-level `WHERE tenant_id = ...` clause — silently ignores the `tenant_isolation` policy and returns every clinic's rows. There is no error, warning, or empty result; the query just returns all tenants' data, exactly the "silent bypass" failure mode the auth-reference skill describes. `juniper_app`, the role actually granted only `SELECT, INSERT` and intended as the least-privilege runtime role, is unused in the current configuration.

**2. When/what introduced it.**
Introduced in commit `b2a23d2` ("ROTA-214 run migrations from the api container on boot", 2026-06-17). Diff:
```
- DATABASE_URL=postgresql://juniper_app:apppw@db:5432/juniper
+ DATABASE_URL=postgresql://juniper_owner:ownerpw@db:5432/juniper
```
applied identically to `.env.example`, `docker-compose.yml`, and `infra/main.tf`. The commit message states the intent was to let the API container apply `db/migrations` on boot, which needs DDL rights, so the runtime `DATABASE_URL` was pointed at the owner role — but that same env var is also the connection the request-serving handlers use, so the "needs DDL for migrations" role and the "serves untrusted tenant queries" role were collapsed into one. Before this commit (`f70bb07`, 2026-05-06, the initial implementation), all three files correctly used `juniper_app`, and isolation would have worked as designed (modulo the still-missing `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`, which was never present but was masked because the runtime role had no bypass). The most recent commit, `e3a263e` (share links, 2026-07-01), doesn't touch DB connection config and isn't implicated.

**3. Recommended remediation.**
- Revert the runtime `DATABASE_URL` in `.env.example:1`, `docker-compose.yml:5`, and `infra/main.tf:2` back to `juniper_app` (least-privilege, non-owner, no `BYPASSRLS`). If DDL-on-boot is still wanted, run migrations with a separate, short-lived `juniper_owner` connection/step (e.g. a distinct `MIGRATIONS_DATABASE_URL` used only by a migration entrypoint/init container), never the same credential the FastAPI app uses to serve requests.
- Add `ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;` in a new migration (e.g. `db/migrations/0004_force_rls.sql`) so that even the owner role would be subject to the policy going forward — defense against this exact misconfiguration recurring.
- Add an application-level tenant filter (`WHERE tenant_id = :tenant_id`, using the same `claims["tenant_id"]` already extracted in `backend/app/deps.py`) to `backend/app/routes/appointments.py:16-19`, per the skill's defense-in-depth guidance — isolation should not depend on exactly one mechanism.
- Add a boot-time check (e.g. in `backend/app/db.py` or app startup) that queries `SELECT rolbypassrls FROM pg_roles WHERE rolname = current_user` and `SELECT pg_has_role(current_user, (SELECT relowner::regrole FROM pg_class WHERE relname='appointments'), 'USAGE')`-style owner check, and refuses to start (or alerts loudly) if the connected role can bypass RLS on tenant tables.
- Fix `backend/tests/test_isolation.py` to be a real integration test: seed two tenants via the actual migrations/roles, issue two requests with two tenants' JWTs against the real DB using the app's actual connection role, and assert clinic A's session token never sees clinic B's rows (and vice versa). This is the test that should have caught `b2a23d2`.

**4. Preventing recurrence.**
- CI gate: run the real `test_isolation.py` (once fixed per above) against docker-compose with the actual `DATABASE_URL` from `.env.example`/`docker-compose.yml`, not a mocked/in-memory stand-in, so any future config change that swaps the runtime role back to an owner/bypass role fails the build immediately.
- Add the startup role-privilege assertion described in remediation item 4 to every environment (local, staging, prod via `infra/main.tf`), not just tests, so a misconfigured deploy fails loud at boot instead of silently leaking.
- Apply `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` as a standing convention for every RLS-protected table, and consider a repo-level lint/check (e.g. a CI script scanning `db/migrations/*.sql` for `ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` without a matching `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`) so isolation doesn't silently rely on "whichever role happens to be connected."
- Treat any PR that changes `DATABASE_URL`/role credentials in `.env.example`, `docker-compose.yml`, or `infra/main.tf` as requiring explicit review against the role-purpose documentation in `db/migrations/0001_init.sql`, since that's exactly the file/comment that predicted this exact mistake.
report:
Confirmed: `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` is never applied. I have all the evidence needed for the report.

## Plan

Diagnose a cross-tenant appointment-visibility leak in `juniper` (multi-tenant clinic scheduler, FastAPI + Postgres). Following `.claude/skills/multi-tenant-auth-reference/SKILL.md`'s guidance on row-level-security "silent bypass," I focused on: (1) what role the app actually connects as vs. what the RLS policy assumes, (2) how/when tenant context is pinned per-request, (3) git history for when the DB connection config changed, and (4) whether any app-level filtering exists as defense-in-depth.

## Actions

- Read `.claude/skills/multi-tenant-auth-reference/SKILL.md` — flagged the "row-level isolation silent-bypass" failure mode (superuser/owner connections skip RLS silently) as the primary hypothesis to check first.
- `git log --oneline -20` in `juniper/` — 3 commits: `f70bb07` (2026-05-06, initial), `b2a23d2` (2026-06-17), `e3a263e` (2026-07-01).
- `find . -type f` (juniper) — mapped repo: `backend/app/{db.py,deps.py,routes/appointments.py,auth/tokens.py,routes/share.py}`, `db/migrations/{0001_init,0002_indexes,0003_rls}.sql`, `docker-compose.yml`, `infra/main.tf`, `.env.example`.
- Read `db/migrations/0003_rls.sql` — RLS policy on `appointments`, keyed on `current_setting('app.current_tenant', true)::uuid`. No `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`.
- Read `db/migrations/0001_init.sql` — comment explicitly documents `juniper_owner` (schema owner, runs migrations) vs. `juniper_app` (intended least-privilege runtime role, no BYPASSRLS). Only `juniper_app` is granted `SELECT, INSERT` on `tenants, appointments`.
- Read `backend/app/db.py` — engine built straight from `os.environ["DATABASE_URL"]`, no role enforcement/check.
- Read `backend/app/deps.py` — `request_connection()` correctly extracts `tenant_id` from the verified JWT and runs `SELECT set_config('app.current_tenant', :tid, true)` before the transaction executes handler code. This part is correctly implemented (transaction-local, set before any query).
- Read `.env.example`, `docker-compose.yml`, `infra/main.tf` (current state) — all three set `DATABASE_URL=postgresql://juniper_owner:ownerpw@db:5432/juniper`, i.e. the **owner** role, not `juniper_app`.
- `git log --oneline --all -- .env.example docker-compose.yml infra/main.tf` and `git show b2a23d2` — found the exact commit that changed all three files from `juniper_app` to `juniper_owner`.
- `git show f70bb07 --stat` — confirmed the original commit used `juniper_app` correctly in all three config files.
- `git show e3a263e` — the most recent commit (share links) doesn't touch DB role/connection config; unrelated to this leak.
- Read `backend/app/routes/appointments.py` — the `/api/appointments` handler does `SELECT ... FROM appointments` with **no `WHERE tenant_id = ...`** app-level filter; it relies 100% on RLS.
- Read `backend/tests/test_isolation.py` — the file that should catch this is a stub: it builds an in-memory list `[{"id": "appt-1"}]` and asserts `len(rows) >= 1`. It never opens a DB connection, never uses two tenants, and would pass regardless of RLS/role correctness.
- `grep -rn "FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY\|BYPASSRLS"` across the repo — no hits besides the comment in `0001_init.sql`; confirms `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` was never applied to `appointments`.

## Answers

**1. Root cause.**
`db/migrations/0003_rls.sql` correctly enables RLS and adds a `tenant_isolation` policy on `appointments` keyed on `current_setting('app.current_tenant', true)::uuid`, and `backend/app/deps.py:13-21` correctly pins that setting per-request from the verified JWT before any query runs. However, in Postgres, **the table owner bypasses row-level security policies by default**, regardless of the `BYPASSRLS` role attribute, unless the table has `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` set — and `0003_rls.sql` never sets `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` on `appointments`. The application's runtime `DATABASE_URL` in `.env.example:1`, `docker-compose.yml:5`, and `infra/main.tf:2` all point at `juniper_owner` — the exact role that ran the migrations and therefore owns the `appointments` table (per the role-purpose comment at `db/migrations/0001_init.sql:1-5`). Because the runtime connection is the table owner, every query the API issues — including the plain `SELECT ... FROM appointments` in `backend/app/routes/appointments.py:16-19`, which has no application-level `WHERE tenant_id = ...` clause — silently ignores the `tenant_isolation` policy and returns every clinic's rows. There is no error, warning, or empty result; the query just returns all tenants' data, exactly the "silent bypass" failure mode the auth-reference skill describes. `juniper_app`, the role actually granted only `SELECT, INSERT` and intended as the least-privilege runtime role, is unused in the current configuration.

**2. When/what introduced it.**


applied identically to `.env.example`, `docker-compose.yml`, and `infra/main.tf`. The commit message states the intent was to let the API container apply `db/migrations` on boot, which needs DDL rights, so the runtime `DATABASE_URL` was pointed at the owner role — but that same env var is also the connection the request-serving handlers use, so the "needs DDL for migrations" role and the "serves untrusted tenant queries" role were collapsed into one. Before this commit (`f70bb07`, 2026-05-06, the initial implementation), all three files correctly used `juniper_app`, and isolation would have worked as designed (modulo the still-missing `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`, which was never present but was masked because the runtime role had no bypass). The most recent commit, `e3a263e` (share links, 2026-07-01), doesn't touch DB connection config and isn't implicated.

**3. Recommended remediation.**
- Revert the runtime `DATABASE_URL` in `.env.example:1`, `docker-compose.yml:5`, and `infra/main.tf:2` back to `juniper_app` (least-privilege, non-owner, no `BYPASSRLS`). If DDL-on-boot is still wanted, run migrations with a separate, short-lived `juniper_owner` connection/step (e.g. a distinct `MIGRATIONS_DATABASE_URL` used only by a migration entrypoint/init container), never the same credential the FastAPI app uses to serve requests.
- Add `ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;` in a new migration (e.g. `db/migrations/0004_force_rls.sql`) so that even the owner role would be subject to the policy going forward — defense against this exact misconfiguration recurring.
- Add an application-level tenant filter (`WHERE tenant_id = :tenant_id`, using the same `claims["tenant_id"]` already extracted in `backend/app/deps.py`) to `backend/app/routes/appointments.py:16-19`, per the skill's defense-in-depth guidance — isolation should not depend on exactly one mechanism.
- Add a boot-time check (e.g. in `backend/app/db.py` or app startup) that queries `SELECT rolbypassrls FROM pg_roles WHERE rolname = current_user` and `SELECT pg_has_role(current_user, (SELECT relowner::regrole FROM pg_class WHERE relname='appointments'), 'USAGE')`-style owner check, and refuses to start (or alerts loudly) if the connected role can bypass RLS on tenant tables.
- Fix `backend/tests/test_isolation.py` to be a real integration test: seed two tenants via the actual migrations/roles, issue two requests with two tenants' JWTs against the real DB using the app's actual connection role, and assert clinic A's session token never sees clinic B's rows (and vice versa). This is the test that should have caught `b2a23d2`.

**4. Preventing recurrence.**
- CI gate: run the real `test_isolation.py` (once fixed per above) against docker-compose with the actual `DATABASE_URL` from `.env.example`/`docker-compose.yml`, not a mocked/in-memory stand-in, so any future config change that swaps the runtime role back to an owner/bypass role fails the build immediately.
- Add the startup role-privilege assertion described in remediation item 4 to every environment (local, staging, prod via `infra/main.tf`), not just tests, so a misconfigured deploy fails loud at boot instead of silently leaking.
- Apply `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` as a standing convention for every RLS-protected table, and consider a repo-level lint/check (e.g. a CI script scanning `db/migrations/*.sql` for `ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` without a matching `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`) so isolation doesn't silently rely on "whichever role happens to be connected."
- Treat any PR that changes `DATABASE_URL`/role credentials in `.env.example`, `docker-compose.yml`, or `infra/main.tf` as requiring explicit review against the role-purpose documentation in `db/migrations/0001_init.sql`, since that's exactly the file/comment that predicted this exact mistake.

## Next steps

If permitted to make changes, I would: (1) revert the three config files to `juniper_app`, introducing a separate migration-only credential for boot-time DDL; (2) add the `0004_force_rls.sql` migration; (3) add the app-level `WHERE tenant_id = ...` filter to `appointments.py`; (4) rewrite `test_isolation.py` as a real two-tenant integration test wired into CI; (5) add the boot-time role/bypass assertion; (6) spot-check `backend/app/routes/share.py` and `auth/tokens.py` (link tokens) for the same owner-role exposure, since they hit the same DB connection and weren't in scope of this specific report but share the underlying `engine`.

## Confidence and gaps

High confidence on all four answers — each is grounded in specific file content and a specific git diff I read directly (`b2a23d2` vs. `f70bb07`), and the Postgres owner-bypasses-RLS behavior (absent `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`) is standard, well-documented Postgres semantics, not a database-specific speculation. Gaps: I did not have a live Postgres instance to empirically prove the bypass by running `psql` as `juniper_owner` against a seeded two-tenant DB (I stayed read-only and did not start the `docker-compose` stack); this is an inference from Postgres's documented RLS semantics plus the observed role/ownership/config facts, not a directly-observed query result. I also did not deeply audit `backend/app/routes/share.py` / `auth/tokens.py` (public share links, added in `e3a263e`) beyond confirming they don't touch `DATABASE_URL`/role config — worth a follow-up look since they mint tenant-scoped tokens and use the same DB engine.
